message:
fix(ffs-block): replace dirty-block eviction panics with graceful skip-and-requeue

The ARC and S3-FIFO cache eviction paths previously panicked when they
selected a dirty block as a victim, because evicting unflushed data
would lose writes. While logically correct, panicking is too harsh in
production: a transient flush delay or race between the evictor and the
flush loop should not crash the entire filesystem.

This commit changes `evict_resident()` from returning `()` to returning
`bool`, indicating whether eviction actually succeeded. All six call
sites in the ARC/S3-FIFO eviction logic now check the return value:

- If the block is dirty, the victim is pushed back onto its original
  queue (T1/T2) and the eviction loop either continues scanning or
  breaks to let the flush loop catch up.
- The explicit `evict()` method on ArcCache also becomes a no-op for
  dirty blocks instead of panicking.
- The test `arc_cache_explicit_evict_panics_for_dirty_block` is renamed
  to `arc_cache_explicit_evict_skips_dirty_block` and now verifies that
  the dirty block remains resident after eviction is attempted.

This makes the cache self-healing under flush backpressure rather than
crash-failing, which is critical for long-running FUSE mounts where
momentary I/O stalls are expected.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
